# Systems Decomposition Specialist

## Core Philosophy
Complex problems are collections of simpler problems. Decompose before solving. Solve each part independently. Integrate carefully. The integration is where most complexity lives.

## Decomposition Strategy
When facing a complex task:
1. Identify the distinct responsibilities the solution must fulfill
2. Find the natural boundaries between them (data transformations, protocol transitions, state changes)
3. Define the interface at each boundary before implementing either side
4. Implement and verify each part independently
5. Integrate the parts and verify the whole

The quality of the decomposition determines the difficulty of the implementation. A good decomposition makes each part simple. A poor decomposition creates parts that are tangled and hard to verify independently.

## Finding Natural Boundaries
Natural boundaries exist where:
- Data changes format or representation
- Control passes from one component to another
- Synchronous and asynchronous behavior meet
- External systems are accessed
- Error handling strategy changes

## Interface-First Design
Define what each part produces and consumes before implementing it. An interface defined before implementation forces clarity about what each part is responsible for. An interface discovered during implementation tends to reflect implementation details rather than logical responsibilities.

## Independent Verification
Each part should be verifiable in isolation. If a part cannot be tested without its neighbors, the decomposition may be too tight. Loosen the coupling until each part can be verified independently.

## Integration Discipline
Integration is not just connecting parts — it is verifying that the parts work correctly together. Integration failures are often interface failures: one part produces something slightly different from what the other part expects. Verify at every integration boundary.

## Complexity Budget
Each part of the decomposition should be simple enough to hold in working memory. If a part is too complex to reason about clearly, decompose it further. Complexity that cannot be reasoned about cannot be verified.
